sourceRefs.fallbackText: Michael Pratt 5 September 2023
sourceRefs.textQuote: Michael Pratt 5 September 2023

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Earlier in 2023, Go 1.20 shipped a preview of profile-guided optimization (PGO) for users to test. After addressing known limitations in the preview, and with additional refinements thanks to community feedback and contributions, PGO support in Go 1.21 is ready for general production use! See the profile-guided optimization user guide for complete documentat
sourceRefs.textQuote: Earlier in 2023, Go 1.20 shipped a preview of profile-guided optimization (PGO) for users to test. After addressing known limitations in the preview, and with additional refinements thanks to community feedback and contr

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Below we will run through an example of using PGO to improve the performance of an application. Before we get to that, what exactly is “profile-guided optimization”?
sourceRefs.textQuote: Below we will run through an example of using PGO to improve the performance of an application. Before we get to that, what exactly is “profile-guided optimization”?

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When you build a Go binary, the Go compiler performs optimizations to try to generate the best performing binary it can. For example, constant propagation can evaluate constant expressions at compile time, avoiding runtime evaluation cost. Escape analysis avoids heap allocations for locally-scoped objects, avoiding GC overheads. Inlining copies the body of s
sourceRefs.textQuote: When you build a Go binary, the Go compiler performs optimizations to try to generate the best performing binary it can. For example, constant propagation can evaluate constant expressions at compile time, avoiding runti

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Go improves optimizations from release to release, but doing so is no easy task. Some optimizations are tunable, but the compiler can’t just “turn it up to 11” on every optimization because overly aggressive optimizations can actually hurt performance or cause excessive build times. Other optimizations require the compiler to make a judgment call about what
sourceRefs.textQuote: Go improves optimizations from release to release, but doing so is no easy task. Some optimizations are tunable, but the compiler can’t just “turn it up to 11” on every optimization because overly aggressive optimization

sourceRefs.fallbackText: With no definitive information about how the code is used in a production environment, the compiler can operate only on the source code of packages. But we do have a tool to evaluate production behavior: profiling . If we provide a profile to the compiler, it can make more informed decisions: more aggressively optimizing the most frequently used functions, o
sourceRefs.textQuote: With no definitive information about how the code is used in a production environment, the compiler can operate only on the source code of packages. But we do have a tool to evaluate production behavior: profiling . If w

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Using profiles of application behavior for compiler optimization is known as Profile-Guided Optimization (PGO) (also known as Feedback-Directed Optimization (FDO)).
sourceRefs.textQuote: Using profiles of application behavior for compiler optimization is known as Profile-Guided Optimization (PGO) (also known as Feedback-Directed Optimization (FDO)).

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Let’s build a service that converts Markdown to HTML: users upload Markdown source to /render , which returns the HTML conversion. We can use gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/markdown to implement this easily.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Let’s build a service that converts Markdown to HTML: users upload Markdown source to /render , which returns the HTML conversion. We can use gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/markdown to implement this easily.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ go mod init example.com/markdown $ go get gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/markdown@bf3e522c626a
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ go mod init example.com/markdown $ go get gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/markdown@bf3e522c626a

sourceRefs.fallbackText: package main import ( "bytes" "io" "log" "net/http" _ "net/http/pprof" "gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/markdown" ) func render(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { if r.Method != "POST" { http.Error(w, "Only POST allowed", http.StatusMethodNotAllowed) return } src, err := io.ReadAll(r.Body) if err != nil { log.Printf("error reading body: %v", err) http.Er
sourceRefs.textQuote: package main import ( "bytes" "io" "log" "net/http" _ "net/http/pprof" "gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/markdown" ) func render(w http.ResponseWriter, r *http.Request) { if r.Method != "POST" { http.Error(w, "Only POST allo

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Build and run the server:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Build and run the server:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ go build -o markdown.nopgo.exe $ ./markdown.nopgo.exe 2023/08/23 03:55:51 Serving on port 8080...
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ go build -o markdown.nopgo.exe $ ./markdown.nopgo.exe 2023/08/23 03:55:51 Serving on port 8080...

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Let’s try sending some Markdown from another terminal. We can use the README.md from the Go project as a sample document:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Let’s try sending some Markdown from another terminal. We can use the README.md from the Go project as a sample document:

sourceRefs.fallbackText:
$ curl -o README.md -L "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/golang/go/c16c2c49e2fa98ae551fc6335215fadd62d33542/README.md" $ curl --data-binary @README.md http://localhost:8080/render

The Go Programming Language

Go is an open source programming language that makes it easy to build simple, reliable, and efficient software.

...
sourceRefs.textQuote:
$ curl -o README.md -L "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/golang/go/c16c2c49e2fa98ae551fc6335215fadd62d33542/README.md" $ curl --data-binary @README.md http://localhost:8080/render

The Go Programming Language

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Now that we have a working service, let’s collect a profile and rebuild with PGO to see if we get better performance.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Now that we have a working service, let’s collect a profile and rebuild with PGO to see if we get better performance.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In main.go , we imported net/http/pprof which automatically adds a /debug/pprof/profile endpoint to the server for fetching a CPU profile.
sourceRefs.textQuote: In main.go , we imported net/http/pprof which automatically adds a /debug/pprof/profile endpoint to the server for fetching a CPU profile.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Normally you want to collect a profile from your production environment so that the compiler gets a representative view of behavior in production. Since this example doesn’t have a “production” environment, I have created a simple program to generate load while we collect a profile. Fetch and start the load generator (make sure the server is still running!):
sourceRefs.textQuote: Normally you want to collect a profile from your production environment so that the compiler gets a representative view of behavior in production. Since this example doesn’t have a “production” environment, I have create

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ go run github.com/prattmic/markdown-pgo/load@latest
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ go run github.com/prattmic/markdown-pgo/load@latest

sourceRefs.fallbackText: While that is running, download a profile from the server:
sourceRefs.textQuote: While that is running, download a profile from the server:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ curl -o cpu.pprof "http://localhost:8080/debug/pprof/profile?seconds=30"
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ curl -o cpu.pprof "http://localhost:8080/debug/pprof/profile?seconds=30"

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Once this completes, kill the load generator and the server.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Once this completes, kill the load generator and the server.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The Go toolchain will automatically enable PGO when it finds a profile named default.pgo in the main package directory. Alternatively, the -pgo flag to go build takes a path to a profile to use for PGO.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The Go toolchain will automatically enable PGO when it finds a profile named default.pgo in the main package directory. Alternatively, the -pgo flag to go build takes a path to a profile to use for PGO.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We recommend committing default.pgo files to your repository. Storing profiles alongside your source code ensures that users automatically have access to the profile simply by fetching the repository (either via the version control system, or via go get ) and that builds remain reproducible.
sourceRefs.textQuote: We recommend committing default.pgo files to your repository. Storing profiles alongside your source code ensures that users automatically have access to the profile simply by fetching the repository (either via the vers

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ mv cpu.pprof default.pgo $ go build -o markdown.withpgo.exe
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ mv cpu.pprof default.pgo $ go build -o markdown.withpgo.exe

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We can check that PGO was enabled in the build with go version :
sourceRefs.textQuote: We can check that PGO was enabled in the build with go version :

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ go version -m markdown.withpgo.exe ./markdown.withpgo.exe: go1.21.0 ... build -pgo=/tmp/pgo121/default.pgo
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ go version -m markdown.withpgo.exe ./markdown.withpgo.exe: go1.21.0 ... build -pgo=/tmp/pgo121/default.pgo

sourceRefs.fallbackText: We will use a Go benchmark version of the load generator to evaluate the effect of PGO on performance.
sourceRefs.textQuote: We will use a Go benchmark version of the load generator to evaluate the effect of PGO on performance.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: First, we will benchmark the server without PGO. Start that server:
sourceRefs.textQuote: First, we will benchmark the server without PGO. Start that server:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ ./markdown.nopgo.exe
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ ./markdown.nopgo.exe

sourceRefs.fallbackText: While that is running, run several benchmark iterations:
sourceRefs.textQuote: While that is running, run several benchmark iterations:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ go get github.com/prattmic/markdown-pgo@latest $ go test github.com/prattmic/markdown-pgo/load -bench=. -count=40 -source $(pwd)/README.md > nopgo.txt
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ go get github.com/prattmic/markdown-pgo@latest $ go test github.com/prattmic/markdown-pgo/load -bench=. -count=40 -source $(pwd)/README.md > nopgo.txt

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Once that completes, kill the original server and start the version with PGO:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Once that completes, kill the original server and start the version with PGO:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ ./markdown.withpgo.exe
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ ./markdown.withpgo.exe

sourceRefs.fallbackText: While that is running, run several benchmark iterations:
sourceRefs.textQuote: While that is running, run several benchmark iterations:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ go test github.com/prattmic/markdown-pgo/load -bench=. -count=40 -source $(pwd)/README.md > withpgo.txt
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ go test github.com/prattmic/markdown-pgo/load -bench=. -count=40 -source $(pwd)/README.md > withpgo.txt

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Once that completes, let’s compare the results:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Once that completes, let’s compare the results:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ go install golang.org/x/perf/cmd/benchstat@latest $ benchstat nopgo.txt withpgo.txt goos: linux goarch: amd64 pkg: github.com/prattmic/markdown-pgo/load cpu: Intel(R) Xeon(R) W-2135 CPU @ 3.70GHz │ nopgo.txt │ withpgo.txt │ │ sec/op │ sec/op vs base │ Load-12 374.5µ ± 1% 360.2µ ± 0% -3.83% (p=0.000 n=40)
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ go install golang.org/x/perf/cmd/benchstat@latest $ benchstat nopgo.txt withpgo.txt goos: linux goarch: amd64 pkg: github.com/prattmic/markdown-pgo/load cpu: Intel(R) Xeon(R) W-2135 CPU @ 3.70GHz │ nopgo.txt │ withpgo.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The new version is around 3.8% faster! In Go 1.21, workloads typically get between 2% and 7% CPU usage improvements from enabling PGO. Profiles contain a wealth of information about application behavior and Go 1.21 just begins to crack the surface by using this information for a limited set of optimizations. Future releases will continue improving performanc
sourceRefs.textQuote: The new version is around 3.8% faster! In Go 1.21, workloads typically get between 2% and 7% CPU usage improvements from enabling PGO. Profiles contain a wealth of information about application behavior and Go 1.21 just

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In this example, after collecting a profile, we rebuilt our server using the exact same source code used in the original build. In a real-world scenario, there is always ongoing development. So we may collect a profile from production, which is running last week’s code, and use it to build with today’s source code. That is perfectly fine! PGO in Go can handl
sourceRefs.textQuote: In this example, after collecting a profile, we rebuilt our server using the exact same source code used in the original build. In a real-world scenario, there is always ongoing development. So we may collect a profile f

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For much more information on using PGO, best practices and caveats to be aware of, please see the profile-guided optimization user guide . If you are curious about what is going on under the hood, keep reading!
sourceRefs.textQuote: For much more information on using PGO, best practices and caveats to be aware of, please see the profile-guided optimization user guide . If you are curious about what is going on under the hood, keep reading!

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To get a better understanding of what made this application faster, let’s take a look under the hood to see how performance has changed. We are going to take a look at two different PGO-driven optimizations.
sourceRefs.textQuote: To get a better understanding of what made this application faster, let’s take a look under the hood to see how performance has changed. We are going to take a look at two different PGO-driven optimizations.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: To observe inlining improvements, let’s analyze this markdown application both with and without PGO.
sourceRefs.textQuote: To observe inlining improvements, let’s analyze this markdown application both with and without PGO.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: I will compare this using a technique called differential profiling, where we collect two profiles (one with PGO and one without) and compare them. For differential profiling, it’s important that both profiles represent the same amount of work , not the same amount of time, so I’ve adjusted the server to automatically collect profiles, and the load generator
sourceRefs.textQuote: I will compare this using a technique called differential profiling, where we collect two profiles (one with PGO and one without) and compare them. For differential profiling, it’s important that both profiles represent

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The changes I have made to the server as well as the profiles collected can be found at https://github.com/prattmic/markdown-pgo . The load generator was run with -count=300000 -quit .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The changes I have made to the server as well as the profiles collected can be found at https://github.com/prattmic/markdown-pgo . The load generator was run with -count=300000 -quit .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: As a quick consistency check, let’s take a look at the total CPU time required to handle all 300k requests:
sourceRefs.textQuote: As a quick consistency check, let’s take a look at the total CPU time required to handle all 300k requests:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ go tool pprof -top cpu.nopgo.pprof | grep "Total samples" Duration: 116.92s, Total samples = 118.73s (101.55%) $ go tool pprof -top cpu.withpgo.pprof | grep "Total samples" Duration: 113.91s, Total samples = 115.03s (100.99%)
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ go tool pprof -top cpu.nopgo.pprof | grep "Total samples" Duration: 116.92s, Total samples = 118.73s (101.55%) $ go tool pprof -top cpu.withpgo.pprof | grep "Total samples" Duration: 113.91s, Total samples = 115.03s (1

sourceRefs.fallbackText: CPU time dropped from ~118s to ~115s, or about 3%. This is in line with our benchmark results, which is a good sign that these profiles are representative.
sourceRefs.textQuote: CPU time dropped from ~118s to ~115s, or about 3%. This is in line with our benchmark results, which is a good sign that these profiles are representative.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Now we can open a differential profile to look for savings:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Now we can open a differential profile to look for savings:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ go tool pprof -diff_base cpu.nopgo.pprof cpu.withpgo.pprof File: markdown.profile.withpgo.exe Type: cpu Time: Aug 28, 2023 at 10:26pm (EDT) Duration: 230.82s, Total samples = 118.73s (51.44%) Entering interactive mode (type "help" for commands, "o" for options) (pprof) top -cum Showing nodes accounting for -0.10s, 0.084% of 118.73s total Dropped 268 nodes
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ go tool pprof -diff_base cpu.nopgo.pprof cpu.withpgo.pprof File: markdown.profile.withpgo.exe Type: cpu Time: Aug 28, 2023 at 10:26pm (EDT) Duration: 230.82s, Total samples = 118.73s (51.44%) Entering interactive mode

sourceRefs.fallbackText: When specifying pprof -diff_base , the values in displayed in pprof are the difference between the two profiles. So, for instance, runtime.scanobject used 0.46s less CPU time with PGO than without. On the other hand, gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/markdown.performReplacements used 0.36s more CPU time. In a differential profile, we typically want to look at the
sourceRefs.textQuote: When specifying pprof -diff_base , the values in displayed in pprof are the difference between the two profiles. So, for instance, runtime.scanobject used 0.46s less CPU time with PGO than without. On the other hand, git

sourceRefs.fallbackText: top -cum shows the top differences by cumulative change. That is, the difference in CPU of a function and all transitive callees from that function. This will generally show the outermost frames in our program’s call graph, such as main or another goroutine entry point. Here we can see most savings are coming from the ruleLinkify portion of handling HTTP req
sourceRefs.textQuote: top -cum shows the top differences by cumulative change. That is, the difference in CPU of a function and all transitive callees from that function. This will generally show the outermost frames in our program’s call gra

sourceRefs.fallbackText: top shows the top differences limited only to changes in the function itself. This will generally show inner frames in our program’s call graph, where most of the actual work is happening. Here we can see that individual savings are coming mostly from runtime functions.
sourceRefs.textQuote: top shows the top differences limited only to changes in the function itself. This will generally show inner frames in our program’s call graph, where most of the actual work is happening. Here we can see that individual

sourceRefs.fallbackText: What are those? Let’s peek up the call stack to see where they come from:
sourceRefs.textQuote: What are those? Let’s peek up the call stack to see where they come from:

(pprof) peek scanobject$ Showing nodes accounting for -3.72s, 3.13% of 118.73s total ----------------------------------------------------------+------------- flat flat% sum% cum cum% calls calls% + context --------------

sourceRefs.fallbackText: So runtime.scanobject is ultimately coming from runtime.gcBgMarkWorker . The Go GC Guide tells us that runtime.gcBgMarkWorker is part of the garbage collector, so runtime.scanobject savings must be GC savings. What about nextFreeFast and other runtime functions?
sourceRefs.textQuote: So runtime.scanobject is ultimately coming from runtime.gcBgMarkWorker . The Go GC Guide tells us that runtime.gcBgMarkWorker is part of the garbage collector, so runtime.scanobject savings must be GC savings. What about

(pprof) peek nextFreeFast$ Showing nodes accounting for -3.72s, 3.13% of 118.73s total ----------------------------------------------------------+------------- flat flat% sum% cum cum% calls calls% + context ------------

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Looks like nextFreeFast and some of the others in the top 10 are ultimately coming from runtime.mallocgc , which the GC Guide tells us is the memory allocator.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Looks like nextFreeFast and some of the others in the top 10 are ultimately coming from runtime.mallocgc , which the GC Guide tells us is the memory allocator.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Reduced costs in the GC and allocator imply that we are allocating less overall. Let’s take a look at the heap profiles for insight:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Reduced costs in the GC and allocator imply that we are allocating less overall. Let’s take a look at the heap profiles for insight:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ go tool pprof -sample_index=alloc_objects -diff_base heap.nopgo.pprof heap.withpgo.pprof File: markdown.profile.withpgo.exe Type: alloc_objects Time: Aug 28, 2023 at 10:28pm (EDT) Entering interactive mode (type "help" for commands, "o" for options) (pprof) top Showing nodes accounting for -12044903, 8.29% of 145309950 total Dropped 60 nodes (cum <= 726549
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ go tool pprof -sample_index=alloc_objects -diff_base heap.nopgo.pprof heap.withpgo.pprof File: markdown.profile.withpgo.exe Type: alloc_objects Time: Aug 28, 2023 at 10:28pm (EDT) Entering interactive mode (type "help"

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The -sample_index=alloc_objects option is showing us the count of allocations, regardless of size. This is useful since we are investigating a decrease in CPU usage, which tends to correlate more with allocation count rather than size. There are quite a few reductions here, but let’s focus on the biggest reduction, mdurl.Parse .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The -sample_index=alloc_objects option is showing us the count of allocations, regardless of size. This is useful since we are investigating a decrease in CPU usage, which tends to correlate more with allocation count ra

sourceRefs.fallbackText: For reference, let’s look at the total allocation counts for this function without PGO:
sourceRefs.textQuote: For reference, let’s look at the total allocation counts for this function without PGO:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ go tool pprof -sample_index=alloc_objects -top heap.nopgo.pprof | grep mdurl.Parse 4974135 3.42% 68.60% 4974135 3.42% gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/mdurl.Parse
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ go tool pprof -sample_index=alloc_objects -top heap.nopgo.pprof | grep mdurl.Parse 4974135 3.42% 68.60% 4974135 3.42% gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/mdurl.Parse

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The total count before was 4974135, meaning that mdurl.Parse has eliminated 100% of allocations!
sourceRefs.textQuote: The total count before was 4974135, meaning that mdurl.Parse has eliminated 100% of allocations!

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Back in the differential profile, let’s gather a bit more context:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Back in the differential profile, let’s gather a bit more context:

(pprof) peek mdurl.Parse Showing nodes accounting for -12257184, 8.44% of 145309950 total ----------------------------------------------------------+------------- flat flat% sum% cum cum% calls calls% + context ---------

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The calls to mdurl.Parse are coming from markdown.normalizeLink and markdown.normalizeLinkText .
sourceRefs.textQuote: The calls to mdurl.Parse are coming from markdown.normalizeLink and markdown.normalizeLinkText .

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (pprof) list mdurl.Parse Total: 145309950 ROUTINE ======================== gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/mdurl.Parse in /usr/local/google/home/mpratt/go/pkg/mod/gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/mdurl@v0.0.0-20191124015652-932350d1cb84/parse .go -4974135 -4974135 (flat, cum) 3.42% of Total . . 60:func Parse(rawurl string) (*URL, error) { . . 61: n, err := findSche
sourceRefs.textQuote: (pprof) list mdurl.Parse Total: 145309950 ROUTINE ======================== gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/mdurl.Parse in /usr/local/google/home/mpratt/go/pkg/mod/gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/mdurl@v0.0.0-20191124015652-932

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Full source for these functions and callers can be found at:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Full source for these functions and callers can be found at:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: markdown.normalizeLink
sourceRefs.textQuote: markdown.normalizeLink

sourceRefs.fallbackText: markdown.normalizeLinkText
sourceRefs.textQuote: markdown.normalizeLinkText

sourceRefs.fallbackText: So what happened here? In a non-PGO build, mdurl.Parse is considered too large to be eligible for inlining. However, because our PGO profile indicated that the calls to this function were hot, the compiler did inline them. We can see this from the “(inline)” annotation in the profiles:
sourceRefs.textQuote: So what happened here? In a non-PGO build, mdurl.Parse is considered too large to be eligible for inlining. However, because our PGO profile indicated that the calls to this function were hot, the compiler did inline the

sourceRefs.fallbackText: $ go tool pprof -top cpu.nopgo.pprof | grep mdurl.Parse 0.36s 0.3% 63.76% 2.75s 2.32% gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/mdurl.Parse $ go tool pprof -top cpu.withpgo.pprof | grep mdurl.Parse 0.55s 0.48% 58.12% 2.03s 1.76% gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/mdurl.Parse (inline)
sourceRefs.textQuote: $ go tool pprof -top cpu.nopgo.pprof | grep mdurl.Parse 0.36s 0.3% 63.76% 2.75s 2.32% gitlab.com/golang-commonmark/mdurl.Parse $ go tool pprof -top cpu.withpgo.pprof | grep mdurl.Parse 0.55s 0.48% 58.12% 2.03s 1.76% gitl

sourceRefs.fallbackText: mdurl.Parse creates a URL as a local variable on line 66 ( var url URL ), and then returns a pointer to that variable on line 145 ( return &url, nil ). Normally this requires the variable to be allocated on the heap, as a reference to it lives beyond function return. However, once mdurl.Parse is inlined into markdown.normalizeLink , the compiler can observe
sourceRefs.textQuote: mdurl.Parse creates a URL as a local variable on line 66 ( var url URL ), and then returns a pointer to that variable on line 145 ( return &url, nil ). Normally this requires the variable to be allocated on the heap, as

sourceRefs.fallbackText: The second largest reduction shown in the profile, from mdurl.(*URL).String is a similar case of eliminating an escape after inlining.
sourceRefs.textQuote: The second largest reduction shown in the profile, from mdurl.(*URL).String is a similar case of eliminating an escape after inlining.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In these cases, we got improved performance through fewer heap allocations. Part of the power of PGO and compiler optimizations in general is that effects on allocations are not part of the compiler’s PGO implementation at all. The only change that PGO made was to allow inlining of these hot function calls. All of the effects to escape analysis and heap allo
sourceRefs.textQuote: In these cases, we got improved performance through fewer heap allocations. Part of the power of PGO and compiler optimizations in general is that effects on allocations are not part of the compiler’s PGO implementation

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In addition to inling, which we saw in the example above, PGO can also drive conditional devirtualization of interface calls.
sourceRefs.textQuote: In addition to inling, which we saw in the example above, PGO can also drive conditional devirtualization of interface calls.

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Before getting to PGO-driven devirtualization, let’s step back and define “devirtualization” in general. Suppose you have code that looks like something like this:
sourceRefs.textQuote: Before getting to PGO-driven devirtualization, let’s step back and define “devirtualization” in general. Suppose you have code that looks like something like this:

sourceRefs.fallbackText: f, _ := os.Open("foo.txt") var r io.Reader = f r.Read(b)
sourceRefs.textQuote: f, _ := os.Open("foo.txt") var r io.Reader = f r.Read(b)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Here we have a call to the io.Reader interface method Read . Since interfaces can have multiple implementations, the compiler generates an indirect function call, meaning it looks up the correct method to call at run time from the type in the interface value. Indirect calls have a small additional runtime cost compared to direct calls, but more importantly t
sourceRefs.textQuote: Here we have a call to the io.Reader interface method Read . Since interfaces can have multiple implementations, the compiler generates an indirect function call, meaning it looks up the correct method to call at run tim

sourceRefs.fallbackText: But in the example above, we do know the concrete method implementation. It must be os.(*File).Read , since *os.File is the only type that could possibly be assigned to r . In this case, the compiler will perform devirtualization , where it replaces the indirect call to io.Reader.Read with a direct call to os.(*File).Read , thus allowing other optimizations.
sourceRefs.textQuote: But in the example above, we do know the concrete method implementation. It must be os.(*File).Read , since *os.File is the only type that could possibly be assigned to r . In this case, the compiler will perform devirtu

sourceRefs.fallbackText: (You are probably thinking “that code is useless, why would anyone write it that way?” This is a good point, but note that code like above could be the result of inlining. Suppose f is passed into a function that takes an io.Reader argument. Once the function is inlined, now the io.Reader becomes concrete.)

sourceRefs.fallbackText: PGO-driven devirtualization extends this concept to situations where the concrete type is not statically known, but profiling can show that, for example, an io.Reader.Read call targets os.(*File).Read most of the time. In this case, PGO can replace r.Read(b) with something like:
sourceRefs.textQuote: PGO-driven devirtualization extends this concept to situations where the concrete type is not statically known, but profiling can show that, for example, an io.Reader.Read call targets os.(*File).Read most of the time. I

sourceRefs.fallbackText: if f, ok := r.(*os.File); ok { f.Read(b) } else { r.Read(b) }
sourceRefs.textQuote: if f, ok := r.(*os.File); ok { f.Read(b) } else { r.Read(b) }

sourceRefs.fallbackText: That is, we add a runtime check for the concrete type that is most likely to appear, and if so use a concrete call, or otherwise fall back to the standard indirect call. The advantage here is that the common path (using *os.File ) can be inlined and have additional optimizations applied, but we still maintain a fallback path because a profile is not a guaran
sourceRefs.textQuote: That is, we add a runtime check for the concrete type that is most likely to appear, and if so use a concrete call, or otherwise fall back to the standard indirect call. The advantage here is that the common path (using

sourceRefs.fallbackText: In our analysis of the markdown server we didn’t see PGO-driven devirtualization, but we also only looked at the top impacted areas. PGO (and most compiler optimizations) generally yield their benefit in the aggregate of very small improvements in lots of different places, so there is likely more happening than just what we looked at.
sourceRefs.textQuote: In our analysis of the markdown server we didn’t see PGO-driven devirtualization, but we also only looked at the top impacted areas. PGO (and most compiler optimizations) generally yield their benefit in the aggregate of

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Inlining and devirtualization are the two PGO-driven optimizations available in Go 1.21, but as we’ve seen, these often unlock additional optimizations. In addition, future versions of Go will continue to improve PGO with additional optimizations.
sourceRefs.textQuote: Inlining and devirtualization are the two PGO-driven optimizations available in Go 1.21, but as we’ve seen, these often unlock additional optimizations. In addition, future versions of Go will continue to improve PGO wit

sourceRefs.fallbackText: Adding profile-guided optimization to Go has been a team effort, and I particularly want to call out the contributions from Raj Barik and Jin Lin at Uber, and Cherry Mui and Austin Clements at Google. This kind of cross-community collaboration is a key part of making Go great.
